docs.title: 🎓 Welcome to the Student Dropout Prediction System
docs.text: An intelligent, early-warning system that helps teachers and principals safeguard the future of their students.

docs.title: ❓ The Problem
docs.text:
Every year, countless students drop out of school because they struggle in silence. By the time a teacher or principal realizes a student is severely failing, it is often too late to help them.

Students don't just fail overnight; there is usually a pattern of declining grades across different subjects that goes unnoticed in the busy day-to-day life of a school.

docs.title: 💡 The Solution
docs.text:
What if a computer could look at a student's test scores, find hidden patterns, and alert the teacher before the student fails?

This Student Dropout Prediction System does exactly that. It acts as an artificial intelligence (AI) assistant for educators.

By simply inputting a student's recent test scores, the system uses Machine Learning to: 1. Predict if the student is at risk of dropping out. 2. Explain why they are at risk (e.g., pointing out exactly which subjects are causing trouble). 3. Group students by the severity of their risk, so teachers know who needs urgent help today versus who needs mild tutoring next week.

docs.title: 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Who Is This For?
docs.text:
This system is built with three types of users in mind:

  • 👨‍🏫 Teachers: Can add their students, input test scores, run the AI predictions, and get actionable advice to help struggling learners.
  • 👩‍💼 Principals: Can oversee the entire school, approve teacher accounts, and view beautiful, school-wide statistics to see overall passing/risk trends.
  • 🎒 Students: Can log into their own portal to track their grades and see their own AI prediction results, keeping them motivated and informed.

docs.title: 📖 How to Read This Documentation
docs.text:
We wrote this guide so that anyone, regardless of technical background, can understand how this project works from A to Z!

Here is what you will find in the menu above:

  • 🌟 Features & Roles: A deep dive into exactly what the Principal, Teacher, and Student can click and do in the app.
  • 🧠 How the AI Works: A simple, beginner-friendly explanation of the "magic" behind the predictions (No math degree required!).
  • 🧩 System Architecture: For the curious—how the "Frontend" (what you see) talks to the "Backend" (the brain) and the Database. Explained using simple analogies.
  • 🚀 Setup & Installation: A step-by-step guide to downloading and running this project on your own computer.

docs.title: Ready to learn more?
docs.text: Click Next at the bottom of the page, or select 🌟 Features & Roles from the menu to continue!

docs.title: 🧩 System Architecture
docs.text:
Ever wonder how the app actually works behind the scenes?

Building a web app is a lot like running a restaurant. Let's break down the technical jargon into a simple analogy!

docs.title: 🍔 The Restaurant Analogy
docs.text:
1. Frontend (The Dining Room & Menu):
  2. Tech: React, Vite, Tailwind CSS
  3. This is what you see and click on. Just like a beautiful menu in a restaurant, the frontend is designed to be easy to read and interact with.
  4. Backend (The Waiter):
  5. Tech: Spring Boot, Java
  6. When you click "Run Prediction", the Frontend hands your request to the Backend. The Backend acts like a waiter—it takes your order (the student's scores), walks back to the kitchen, and eventually brings you the result.
  7. Database (The Pantry & Order History):
  8. Tech: MySQL
  9. The waiter needs to know if the student exists or what their past scores were. They look this up in the Database, which is a giant digital filing cabinet holding all the school's records.
  10. ML Service (The Head Chef):
  11. Tech: Flask, Python, Machine Learning
  12. This is the brain! When the waiter brings the scores to the kitchen, the Head Chef (the AI) looks at them, applies complex recipes (algorithms), and says, "This student is at risk of dropping out." The waiter takes this information and brings it back to you!

docs.title: Detailed Component Flow
docs.text:
graph TB
    subgraph "Frontend — React 19 + Vite"
        UI[Browser / User Interface]
    end

    subgraph "Reverse Proxy — Nginx"
        NG[Nginx :80]
    end

    subgraph "Backend — Spring Boot 3.5"
        API[REST API :8080]
        AUTH[AuthController]
        TC[TeacherController]
        PC[PrincipalController]
        SC[StudentController]
        JPA[Hibernate / JPA]
    end

    subgraph "ML Service — Flask / Python"
        ML[Flask API :5000]
        DT[Decision Tree - J48]
        KM[K-Means Clustering]
        AP[Apriori Association Rules]
    end

    subgraph "Database — MySQL 8"
        DB[(dropout_db)]
    end

    UI --> NG
    NG -- "static files" --> UI
    NG -- "/api/*" --> API
    API --> AUTH
    API --> TC
    API --> PC
    API --> SC
    JPA --> DB
    TC -- "POST /ml/predict" --> ML
    TC -- "GET /ml/metrics" --> ML
    TC -- "GET /ml/clusters" --> ML
    TC -- "GET /ml/rules" --> ML
    ML --> DT
    ML --> KM
    ML --> AP

docs.title: 🗄 Database Schema
docs.text:
The database dropout_db has 4 tables, auto-created by Hibernate:

erDiagram
    TEACHERS {
        bigint id PK
        varchar name
        varchar email UK
        varchar password
        enum status "PENDING | APPROVED | REJECTED"
    }

    STUDENTS {
        bigint id PK
        varchar student_id UK
        varchar name
        varchar email UK
        varchar password
        enum status "PENDING | APPROVED | REJECTED"
        bigint teacher_id FK
    }

    SCORES {
        bigint id PK
        bigint student_id FK,UK
        double test_1 "0.0 - 10.0"
        double test_2 "0.0 - 10.0"
        double test_3 "0.0 - 10.0"
        double test_4 "0.0 - 10.0"
        double main_exam "0.0 - 10.0"
        datetime created_at
    }

    PREDICTIONS {
        bigint id PK
        bigint student_id FK,UK
        varchar status "A (Pass) or R (At Risk)"
        int cluster_group "0-3, null if Pass"
        varchar weak_tests "Comma-separated list"
        double pass_probability "0.0 - 1.0"
        double risk_probability "0.0 - 1.0"
        datetime created_at
    }

    TEACHERS ||--o{ STUDENTS : "has many"
    STUDENTS ||--o| SCORES : "has one"
    STUDENTS ||--o| PREDICTIONS : "has one"

docs.title: 🤖 The "Brain" (Machine Learning Pipeline)
docs.text: The system doesn't just guess; it uses three mathematical "recipes" (algorithms) working together:

docs.title: 1. The Decision Maker (J48 Decision Tree)
docs.text:
• What it does: It looks at the 5 test scores and makes a firm decision: Pass or At Risk.
  • Analogy: Imagine a flowchart that says, "If Test 1 is below 7, go left. If Test 2 is above 7, go right." The AI learned this flowchart by studying 700 past students.

docs.title: 2. The Organizer (K-Means Clustering)
docs.text:
• What it does: If a student is marked "At Risk", this algorithm groups them into one of 4 severity levels so teachers know who needs help first.
  • Analogy: It's like sorting laundry into "Delicate," "Normal," and "Heavy Duty." It finds students who have similar struggles and puts them in the same group.
Severity Level What it means 0 Very Low Scorer — needs urgent support in all subjects 1 Borderline Fail — slightly below passing 2 Average Weak — moderate scores but missing the mark 3 Low Main Exam — does okay on tests, but fails the big exam

docs.title: 3. The Pattern Finder (Apriori Association Rules)
docs.text:
• What it does: It looks for hidden relationships between different tests.
  • Analogy: Just like Amazon says "People who bought a flashlight also bought batteries," this algorithm says "Students who fail Test 1 almost always fail Test 3." This helps teachers catch problems early!

docs.title: 📊 Data Flow — How Data Enters the System
Step 1: Principal Logs In (Hardcoded Account)
docs.text:
Email:    principal@school.com
Password: principal123
The principal account is pre-configured in the system code.

docs.title: Step 2: Teacher Registers
docs.text:
• Teacher goes to the Register page and fills in Name, Email, Password
  • Account is created with status PENDING
  • Principal logs in → Teacher Management → Approves the teacher
  • Teacher can now log in

docs.title: Step 3: Teacher Adds Students
docs.text:
• Teacher logs in → Student Management → Adds student (Name, Email, Student ID, Password)
  • Student account is created with status PENDING
  • Teacher approves the student → Student can now log in

docs.title: Step 4: Teacher Enters Test Scores
docs.text:
• Teacher logs in → Score Entry → Selects a student
  • Enters 5 scores (each between 0.0 and 10.0):
  • Test 1, Test 2, Test 3, Test 4, Main Exam

docs.title: Step 5: Teacher Runs Prediction
docs.text:
• Teacher logs in → Predictions → Selects a student → Clicks "Predict"
  • Backend sends the 5 scores to Flask ML → Gets prediction result
  • Result is saved in the database and displayed:
  • Status: Pass or At Risk
  • Probability: Pass % and Risk %
  • Risk Group: (if At Risk) which cluster they belong to
  • Weak Tests: Which tests scored below 7.0
  • Matching Rules: Association rule patterns

docs.title: Step 6: Student Views Their Data
docs.text:
• Student logs in → Dashboard shows their scores and prediction results
  • Students can also update their profile

docs.title: Visual Flow
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Principal
    participant T as Teacher
    participant B as Backend (Spring Boot)
    participant F as Flask ML
    participant DB as MySQL
    participant S as Student

    P->>B: Login (hardcoded)
    T->>B: Register
    B->>DB: Save teacher (PENDING)
    P->>B: Approve teacher
    B->>DB: Update status → APPROVED

    T->>B: Login
    T->>B: Add student
    B->>DB: Save student (PENDING)
    T->>B: Approve student
    B->>DB: Update status → APPROVED

    T->>B: Enter scores (5 tests)
    B->>DB: Save scores

    T->>B: Run prediction
    B->>F: POST /ml/predict (5 scores)
    F->>F: Normalize → Decision Tree → K-Means → Apriori
    F-->>B: Prediction result
    B->>DB: Save prediction
    B-->>T: Display result

    S->>B: Login
    B->>DB: Fetch scores & prediction
    B-->>S: Display dashboard

docs.title: Auth (/api/auth)
docs.text: Method Endpoint Description POST /login Login for all roles (Principal/Teacher/Student) POST /teacher/register Register a new teacher

docs.title: Teacher (/api/teacher)
docs.text: Method Endpoint Description POST /students Add a new student GET /students/{teacherId} Get all students for a teacher PUT /students/approve/{id} Approve student account PUT /students/reject/{id} Reject student account POST /scores Enter test scores for a student POST /predict/{studentId} Run ML prediction on a student GET /predictions/{teacherId} Get all predictions GET /model/metrics Get ML model accuracy metrics GET /clusters Get K-Means cluster information GET /rules Get association rules GET/PUT /profile/{teacherId} View/update teacher profile

docs.title: Principal (/api/principal)
docs.text: Method Endpoint Description GET /teachers Get all teachers GET /teachers/pending Get pending teachers PUT /teachers/approve/{id} Approve teacher account PUT /teachers/reject/{id} Reject teacher account GET /monitor Monitor all students across teachers GET /statistics Get system-wide statistics

docs.title: Student (/api/student)
docs.text: Method Endpoint Description GET /scores/{studentId} View own scores GET /prediction/{studentId} View own prediction GET/PUT /profile/{studentId} View/update profile

docs.title: Flask ML (/ml)
docs.text: Method Endpoint Description POST /predict Run prediction on 5 scores GET /metrics Get model accuracy GET /clusters Get cluster centroids GET /rules Get association rules GET /health Health check

docs.text: Service Image Internal Port Host Port mysql mysql:8.0 3306 3307 flask-ml code-flask-ml 5000 5001 backend code-backend 8080 8080 frontend code-frontend 80 3000

docs.title: 🛠 Tech Stack Summary
docs.text: Layer Technology Frontend React 19, Vite 7, Tailwind CSS 4, React Router 7, Recharts, Chart.js Backend Spring Boot 3.5.11, Java 17, Maven, Hibernate/JPA ML Service Python 3.11, Flask, scikit-learn, NumPy, Gunicorn Database MySQL 8.0 Reverse Proxy Nginx (Alpine) Containerization Docker, Docker Compose

docs.title: 🌟 Features & Roles
docs.text:
This section explains exactly what everyone can do in the Student Dropout Prediction System.

Think of the system as a secure school building. Not everyone has the keys to every room. We use User Roles to make sure the right people see the right information.

docs.title: 👩‍💼 The Principal
docs.text:
The Principal is the administrator of the entire system. They have a bird's-eye view of everything happening across all teachers and all students.

Feature / Action What it means Approve Teachers When a new teacher registers, they cannot log in right away. The Principal must verify they actually work at the school and click "Approve" before the teacher can use the app. Reject Teachers If an unauthorized person tries to register, the Principal can reject them to keep student data safe. School-Wide Statistics The Principal can see a special dashboard showing how many students are passing and how many are "At Risk" across the entire school. Monitor All Students The Principal can search for any student in the school to check their test scores and AI prediction results, no matter which teacher they belong to.

docs.title: 👨‍🏫 The Teacher
docs.text:
The Teacher does the day-to-day work in the app. They manage their own classroom of students and use the AI to help them teach.

Feature / Action What it means Add Students Teachers create accounts for their students. Just like teachers, students need to be "Approved" by the teacher before they can log in. Enter Test Scores The teacher enters grades for 5 exams (Internal Tests 1-4, and the Main Exam). Scores must be between 0.0 and 10.0. Run AI Predictions With the click of a button, the teacher sends the student's scores to the AI. The system instantly replies with whether the student is likely to Pass or is At Risk of dropping out. View Risk Severity If a student is At Risk, the system categorizes them (e.g., "Very Low Scorer" vs "Borderline Fail") so the teacher knows how urgent the situation is. See Weak Tests The app highlights exactly which subjects or tests the student is struggling with the most. View Actionable Rules The AI discovers patterns (like "Students who fail Test 1 and Test 2 almost always fail Test 3"). Teachers can view these rules to intervene early.

docs.title: 🎒 The Student
docs.text:
Students have their own portal! The goal here is transparency and motivation.

Feature / Action What it means View Own Scores Students can log in anytime to see the test scores their teacher has entered for them. View AI Prediction Students can see their own Pass/Risk prediction. If they are marked "At Risk", the dashboard turns red to warn them. If they are marked "Pass", it turns green to encourage them. Update Profile Students can change their name or password to keep their account secure.

docs.title: Understanding the Flow
docs.text:
Here is a simple way to understand how a student goes from being added to getting an AI prediction:

  1. 📝 Teacher Adds Student: The teacher enters the student's name into the system.
  2. ✅ Teacher Approves Student: The student can now log in to the app from their home computer.
  3. 💯 Teacher Enters Scores: As the school year goes on, the teacher types in grades (0 to 10).
  4. 🔮 Teacher Clicks "Predict": The AI looks at the scores and calculates the risk of dropping out.
  5. 📊 Everyone Sees the Result: The Teacher, the Principal, and the Student can all log in to see the results and work together to improve the student's grades!

The goal of these features is to bring principals, teachers, and students together to prevent dropouts.

docs.title: 🚀 Setup & Installation
docs.text:
Want to run this project on your own computer? It's easier than it looks!

Instead of installing 10 different pieces of software (like Java, Python, and MySQL servers), we use Docker. Docker is like a magic moving box—it packs up the entire application and everything it needs into little containers that run perfectly on any computer.

Follow these simple steps:

docs.title: 🛠 What You Need First
docs.text:
Before you start, you only need to download two things:

  1. Git (To download the code to your computer)
  2. Docker Desktop (To run the application boxes)

Make sure Docker Desktop is open and running in the background before you continue!

docs.title: Step 1: Download the Code
docs.text:
Open your computer's terminal (or Command Prompt) and paste this command to download the project:

git clone https://github.com/yxshkm404/student-dropout-prediction.git
cd student-dropout-prediction

docs.title: Step 2: Configure the Database (.env)
docs.text:
You need to set up the database passwords. Create a file named .env in the same folder where you ran the commands above, and add these two lines:

MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=your_secure_password
MYSQL_DATABASE=dropout_db

docs.title: Step 3: Start the Project
docs.text:
Because we are using Docker, starting the project takes exactly one command.

In your terminal, make sure you are in the student-dropout-prediction folder, and run:

docker-compose up --build

Wait about 2-3 minutes. You will see a lot of text scrolling by. This is Docker downloading Java, Python, a database, and setting up the whole system for you!

When the text stops moving rapidly, the app is ready.

docs.title: 🎉 How to View the App
docs.text:
Once Docker is finished building, you can open your web browser (like Chrome or Safari) and go to these links:

  • 🖥 The Main Website (Frontend): http://localhost:3000 (Go here to click around!)
  • ⚙️ The Backend API: http://localhost:8080/api/teacher/model/metrics (API test)
  • 🤖 The AI Server: http://localhost:5001/ml/health (ML test)

docs.title: How to Log In as the Principal
docs.text:
To test out the Principal features, use the account that is built into the system:

  • Email: principal@school.com
  • Password: principal123

(From there, you can approve new Teacher accounts that you create!)

docs.title: 🛑 How to Stop the App
docs.text:
When you are done testing, go back to your terminal where the text is running and press:

Ctrl + C (or Cmd + C on Mac)

If you want to completely erase the containers and start fresh next time (which deletes the mock student data inside the local database), run this command: 

docker-compose down -v

docs.title: 🔮 Prediction Logic — How It Works
docs.text: This document explains how the Student Dropout Prediction system works, including how predictions are made, and how the Risk Percentage and Pass Percentage are calculated.

docs.title: 📊 Overview
docs.text:
The system uses three ML algorithms working together:

Algorithm Purpose Implementation J48 Decision Tree Classifies a student as Pass (A) or At Risk (R) decision_tree_model.pkl K-Means Clustering Groups at-risk students into 4 severity levels kmeans_model.pkl Apriori Association Reveals patterns like "Low Test 1 → Low Test 3" association_rules.json

docs.title: 🔁 End-to-End Prediction Flow
docs.text:
React Frontend                 Spring Boot                   Flask ML API
─────────────                  ───────────                   ────────────
Teacher clicks                 POST /api/teacher             POST /ml/predict
"Run Prediction"  ──────────►  /predict/{studentId}  ──────► (app.py)
                               │                             │
                               │  Reads scores from DB       │  1. Normalize scores
                               │  Sends to Flask             │  2. Decision Tree predict
                               │                             │  3. K-Means cluster (if Risk)
                               │  ◄──────────────────────────│  4. Find weak tests
                               │  Saves Prediction to DB     │  5. Match Apriori rules
                               │                             │  6. Return probabilities
Teacher sees result ◄──────────│

docs.title: 🧪 Input Features
docs.text:
The model takes 5 test scores, each on a scale of 0.0 – 10.0:

Feature Description test_1 Internal Test 1 score test_2 Internal Test 2 score test_3 Internal Test 3 score test_4 Internal Test 4 score main_exam Final/Main Examination score

docs.title: Step 1 — Normalize Scores
docs.text:
scores_array  = np.array([scores_raw])       # e.g. [[4.5, 3.2, 6.1, 5.0, 2.8]]
scores_scaled = scaler.transform(scores_array) # MinMaxScaler normalization

The raw scores (0–10) are normalized using a pre-trained MinMaxScaler (scaler.pkl) so the model sees values in the same range it was trained on.

docs.title: Step 2 — Decision Tree Classification (Pass / At Risk)
docs.text:
prediction = dt_model.predict(scores_scaled)[0]       # 0 or 1
proba      = dt_model.predict_proba(scores_scaled)[0]  # [pass_prob, risk_prob]
status     = le.inverse_transform([prediction])[0]     # 'A' (Pass) or 'R' (At Risk)

The J48 Decision Tree classifier outputs: - A class label: A (Pass) or R (At Risk) - A probability array: [P(Pass), P(At Risk)] — this is where risk% and pass% come from

docs.title: ⭐ How Risk % and Pass % Are Calculated
docs.text:
pass_prob = round(float(proba[0]) * 100, 2)   # → 100.0% or 0.0%
risk_prob = round(float(proba[1]) * 100, 2)   # → 0.0% or 100.0%
Value Source Formula Actual Output Pass % proba[0] from Decision Tree P(class = A) × 100 100.0% or 0.0% Risk % proba[1] from Decision Tree P(class = R) × 100 0.0% or 100.0%

Key point: The Pass % and Risk % are always 100% or 0% — never intermediate values. This is because the Decision Tree's leaf nodes are pure (each leaf contains training samples of only one class). Pass % + Risk % = 100% always.

Why always 100% or 0%? - The Decision Tree has been trained on historical student data (700 students) with a clear 7.0 pass threshold. - When a new student's scores reach a leaf node, the tree counts how many training samples in that leaf were Pass (A) vs At Risk (R). - Because the tree is deep enough and the data separates cleanly at the 7.0 threshold, every leaf node ends up pure — containing 100% of one class. - Example: A leaf might have 50 Pass students and 0 At Risk students → proba = [1.0, 0.0] → Pass% = 100%, Risk% = 0%. - This means the prediction is always a hard classification — either fully Pass or fully At Risk.

docs.title: 🤔 FAQ: Is it wrong that the model gives exactly 100% or 0% risk?
docs.text:
No, it is NOT wrong! It is 100% correct for this specific model and dataset.

You do not have a broken model. Here is why it is completely normal and mathematically correct:

  1. You have cleanly separated data: The dataset has clear rules (e.g., scoring above 7.0 means passing).
  2. Decision Trees are "Confident": A Decision Tree algorithm works by splitting data into groups like a flowchart. Because your data has a clear boundary, the tree was able to keep splitting until every single "bucket" (leaf) at the end of the flowchart contained only passing students or only failing students.
  3. Pure leaves: In Machine Learning, we call these "pure leaves". If a new student's scores drop them into a bucket that had 50 passing students and 0 failing students during training, the math is simple:
  4. Pass % = (50 / 50) * 100 = 100%
  5. Risk % = (0 / 50) * 100 = 0%

It simply means your model has zero doubt about its classification because it has learned exactly what a passing student's scores look like versus an at-risk student's scores.

docs.title: 3️⃣ K-Means Clustering (Only for At-Risk Students)
docs.text:
if status == 'R':
    cluster = int(kmeans.predict(scores_scaled)[0])

If the student is classified as At Risk, K-Means assigns them to one of 4 risk severity clusters:

Cluster Description 0 Very Low Scorer — needs urgent support in all tests 1 Borderline Fail — slightly below passing in all tests 2 Average Weak — moderate scores but below threshold 3 Low Main Exam — weak in main exam and tests

This helps teachers understand what kind of at-risk student they're dealing with.

docs.title: Step 4 — Identify Weak Tests
docs.text:
test_names = ['Test 1', 'Test 2', 'Test 3', 'Test 4', 'Main Exam']
weak_tests = [test_names[i] for i, s in enumerate(scores_raw) if s < 7.0]

Any individual test score below 7.0 (the pass threshold) is flagged as a "weak test". This tells the teacher exactly which subjects the student needs help in.

docs.title: Step 5 — Apriori Association Rule Matching
docs.text:
test_keys = ['test_1_LOW', 'test_2_LOW', 'test_3_LOW', 'test_4_LOW', 'main_exam_LOW']
low_tests = set()
for i, s in enumerate(scores_raw):
    if s < 7.0:
        low_tests.add(test_keys[i])

The system checks against 180+ pre-mined association rules to find patterns like:

"If a student scores low on Test 1 AND Test 2, there is a 98.8% confidence they also score low on Test 3"

Each rule has: - Antecedents: the "if" part (e.g., test_1_LOW, test_2_LOW) - Consequents: the "then" part (e.g., test_3_LOW) - Confidence: how often this pattern holds (e.g., 98.8%)

The top 3 matching rules are returned in the prediction result.

docs.title: Step 6 — Average Score
docs.text:
average = round(sum(scores_raw) / len(scores_raw), 2)

A simple arithmetic mean of all 5 test scores — provided as a quick reference alongside the ML prediction.

docs.title: 📦 Final Prediction Response
docs.text:
When all steps complete, the Flask API returns:

{
  "success": true,
  "status": "R",
  "statusMeaning": "At Risk",
  "average": 4.54,
  "passProbability": 0.0,
  "riskProbability": 100.0,
  "cluster": 0,
  "clusterDescription": "Very Low Scorer - needs urgent support in all tests",
  "weakTests": ["Test 1", "Test 2", "Test 4", "Main Exam"],
  "matchingRules": [
    {
      "antecedents": ["test_1_LOW", "test_2_LOW"],
      "consequents": ["test_3_LOW"],
      "confidence": 98.8
    }
  ]
}

Spring Boot then saves this prediction to the MySQL database and forwards the result to the React frontend.

docs.title: 📈 Model Performance (from model_metadata.json)
docs.text:
Metric Value Accuracy 93.80% Precision 93.80% Recall 98.40% F1 Score 96.10% Cohen's Kappa 0.884 MAE 0.0735 RMSE 0.2267

The model was trained on a dataset of 700 students with scores ranging from 0.0 to 10.0 and a pass threshold of 7.0.

docs.title: 🗂️ Pre-trained Model Files
docs.text:
All model files are stored in BACKEND/demo/src/main/resources/flask-ml/data/:

File Contents decision_tree_model.pkl Trained J48 Decision Tree classifier kmeans_model.pkl Trained K-Means model (4 clusters) scaler.pkl MinMaxScaler fitted on training data label_encoder.pkl Encodes A/R ↔ 0/1 association_rules.json 180+ Apriori rules (support, confidence, lift) model_metadata.json Accuracy, precision, recall, thresholds
